text: BRAMSCHE, a town of Hanover, in the prov. and 10 m. NNW of Osnabrück, on the l. bank of the Hasse. Pop. 1,600. It contains manufactories of linen and cloth, and has a considerable commerce in cattle.
BRAMSHALL, a parish in the co. of Stafford, 2 m. W of Uttoxeter. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 170.
BRAMSHAW, a parish partly in the co. of Wilts, and partly in the co. of Southampton, 6 m. NNW of Lyndhurst. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 793.
BRAMSHILL, a tything in the p. of Eversley, co. of Southampton, 13 m. NW of Hartford Bridge. Pop. 175.
BRAMSHOT, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 4 m. W of Haslemere; including the hamlet of Liphook. Area 6,190 acres. Pop. 1,313.

BRAMSTEDT, a town of Holstein, the cap. of the amt of Segeberg, on the r. bank of the Bram, 25 m. NNW of Hamburg, and 21 m. ENE of Gluckstadt. Pop. 800. There is a custom-house here; and the place has a considerable commerce in cattle. In the environs are several mineral springs and baths.
BRAMWITH (KIRK), a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. W by S of Thorne. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 251.

BRAN, a tributary of the Tay in Perthshire, which issues from the E end of Loch Freuchie in the p. of Dull; flows NE past Amulree and through Strath Bran, or Brand, in the p. of Little Dunkeld; and falls into the Tay a little above Dunkeld-bridge, after a course of about 14 m. It forms romantic falls a little above its junction with the Tay.

BRANAR AND MARCHELED, a chapelry in the p. of Llangerniew, Denbighshire, 7 m. NE of Llanrwst. Pop. in 1801, 127; in 1841, 128. BRA group, between Santo Nicolao and Santa Luzia. It is 21 m. long from SE to NW, and about 6 m. in circumf., and rises in Monte Gardo, to the height of 4,200 ft. above sea-level. It is difficult of approach, and, excepting in a bay on the S side, it is destitute of shelter. On the NW side of the island is a small v., surrounded by most picturesque and magnificent scenery.

BRANCALEONE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, in the dist. and 24 m. SSW of Gerace, on an eminence near the shore of the Ionian sea. Pop. 838. It was, to a great extent, destroyed in 1783 by an earthquake. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.
BRANCA (SIERRA DO SANTA), a mountain-range of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes and Bahia, forming a portion of the great mountain chain by which these prov. are intersected.
BRANCASTER, or Branchester, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. WNW of Burnham-Westgate, on the E shore of St. Edmund's point, possessing a quay for vessels. Area 3,340 acres. Pop. 913.

BRANCEPETH, a parish in the co.-palatine of Durham, 4 m. SW of Durham, and 44 m. W of the Great North of England railway. A branch of the Bishop-Auckland and Weardale railroad, 3 m. 16 ch. in length, terminates at Crook, in this parish. It contains the townships of Brancepeth, Brandon with Byshottles, Crook with Billy-row, Hedley-hope, Hemlington-row, Stockley, and Willington. Area 21,850 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,508; in 1841, 2,151.

BRANCH, a county in the state of Michigan, U. S. It comprises an area of 528 sq. m., and is drained by branches of St. Joseph's river; and the soil consists of a fertile sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 5,715.—The cap., a village of the same name, is situated in the township of and on the W branch of the Coldwater, 113 m. WSW of Detroit.—Also a township of Shuylkill co., in the state of Pennsylvania. It contains extensive mines of anthracite coal. Pop. in 1840, 1,422.

BRANCHIER (SAINT), a parish and town of Switzerland, cap. of the Dixain of Entremont, in the cant. of Valais, on the l. bank of the Dranse, 18 m. SW of Sion. It is situated at the junction of the valleys of Bagnes, Entremont, and Martigny, on the road to the pass of the Great St. Bernard, at an alt. of 2,418 ft. above sea-level. It contains a fine church; and in the environs are the ruins of the ancient fortresses of St. Jean and Etiez, and a convent. Mines of iron and lead exist in the locality.
BRANCH-PORT, a village of Jerusalem township, Yates co., in the state of New York, U. S., on the W branch of Crooked Lake. Pop. in 1840, about 125.
BRANCHS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Montbazon. Pop. 2,010.
BRANCIFORTE, a village of Upper California, 1½ m. from the bay of Monterey. Pop. 150.
BRANCION, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Tournus. Pop. 591.

BRANCO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio Negro, which unites with the Guatuma, 120 m. NW of the junction of that river with the Amazon.—Also a river in the prov. of Bahia, which issues from the Serra Duro, and, uniting with several other streams, forms a tributary of the San Francisco.—Also a river in the prov. of Porto Seguro, a tributary of the Rio San Matheo.
BRANCO, or Paracaima, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, formed by the junction of several large streams which take their rise on the W versant of

BRANCA, a rocky islet in the Cape de Verd | the Serra Paracaima; and which, after a course of 450 m. in a generally SSW direction, falls by four channels into the Rio Negro-of which it forms one of the principal tributaries on the l. bank-200 m. NW of the confluence of that river with the Amazon. By the Indians this river is called the QUE'CUENE'.
BRANCO (CAPE), a promontory on the E coast of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, in Slat. 7° 8' 22", W long. 34° 47′ 58".
BRANCO CASTELLO. See CASTELLO BRANCO.
BRANCO ZERO. See ZERO BRANCO.
BRANCOS (Los), a mountain range of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, running along the 20th parallel of S lat.
BRANCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Bohain. Pop. 1,558.

BRAND, a town of Saxony, 21 m. SW of Dresden, in the bailiwick and 3 m. SSW of Freiberg, near an affluent of the Mulde. Pop. in 1837, 2,178. It contains an hospital for miners; and has manufactories of jewellery, blonde, and wooden-ware. In the vicinity are the silver mines of Himmelfurst, the richest in the kingdom, also mines of lead and arsenic.
BRANDAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Quintin. Pop. 3,342.
BRANDE, a town of Denmark, in the prov. of Jutland, and 25 m. NW of Weile, on an affluent of the Lonborg.

BRANDEIS, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Kaurzim, on the l. bank of the Elbe, 13 m. ENE of Prague. Pop. 2,412. It contains a royal castle, now attached to the military hospital at Prague, a college, a seminary, and about 190 houses. It has an extensive manufactory of printed calicoes; and in the environs clay of a peculiar species is found.

BRANDEIS-AM-ADLER, a town of Bohemia, the cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 33 m. SE of Königgrätz, on the r. bank of the Stille-Adler. Pop. 1,172. It contains a castle; and possesses a manufactory of saltpetre.

BRANDENBURG, an important prov. of Prussia, comprising the ancestral dominions of the reigning family; bounded on the N by Mecklenburg, Pomerania, and Western Prussia; on the E by Posen and Silesia; on the S by the kingdom of Saxony; and on the W by Anhalt-Dessau, Saxony, and Hanover. It lies between the parallels of 51° 20', and 53° 35' N lat.; and 11° 13', and 16° 12' E long. Its area is 730.94 German, or 15,520 English sq. m. Pop. in 1837, with the Baltic. The soil is prevailingly sandy; in some districts it is mixed with lime, clay, and vegetable mould; but there are very extensive heaths and plains entirely covered with quicksand. The few eminences which occur are in the S districts. Rivers. The country is well watered. The Elbe, entering it from Silesia, to the E of Krossen, forms a part of its NW boundaries, from a little below Sandau to the junction of the Elde above Dömitz; and the Oder runs through it in a W, N, NNW, and NNE direction. These two streams receive in their course almost all the other rivers of the country. The principal streams flowing into the Elbe are the Elde, the Stepnitz, and the Havel; the last a navigable river, and of importance to the inland commerce. The principal rivers belonging to the basin of the Oder, are the Bober, the Neisse, and the Wartha, a large navigable river coming from Posen. The inclination of the surface is so small that all these rivers have a gentle course; and in some quarters spread out into lakes. Canals.] Among other obstructions to agriculture and commerce in the Prussian dominions, was the difficulty of conveying materials and merchandise to different parts of the interior. To remove these, and facilitate trade, the great Frederic improved the navigation of several rivers, and caused several canals to be formed. The canal of Plauen shortens the water-passage between Berlin and Magdeburg by about one-half. It begins near Parie or Pasay, on the Elbe; intersects the Elbe and Stremme, having three sluices on it which check the fall from the Elbe into the Havel, 21 ft. in height; and passes by Plauen into the Havel. This canal is 21 m. in length; 22 ft. wide at the bottom; and 26 ft. wide at the surface; and in some places between 40 and 50 ft. broad. -Another canal, joining the Spree and the Oder, was completed between the years 1662 and 1668. It issues out of the Spree into a lake near Muhlrose, in the Middle Mark, and thence runs partly along the Schlubbe, partly through it, towards the Oder, being 13½ m. in length; about 50 ft. wide; and 6 ft. deep. -Another canal, that of Finow, connects the Havel and Oder. It begins at Liebenwalde, on the Havel; passes thence into the river Finow or Fuhne; and runs into the Oder below Lower Finow. It is 25 m. in length.—The Oder canal branches from the Oder, at the village of Gustebiese, and falls into it again about 4½ m. from its first issue.—The number of boats employed upon the canals and rivers of B. at the end of 1837 was <math>2,968 = 130,986</math> tons. Railroads.] B. is intersected by five great lines of railroad, all diverging from Berlin as a common centre. These lines are the Berlin and Stettin, which is to be carried southwards to Posen; the Berlin and Vienna line, with a branch to Glogau; the Berlin, Kolen, and Leipzig line; the Berlin, Magdeburg, and Hanover line; and the Berlin and Altona line, which is continued to Kiel on the Baltic. Lakes.] There are no large lakes, but several small ones, such as those of Ruppin, Soldin, Grimnitz, and Werbdlin. There are several mineral springs; but those of Freienwalde, and Neustadt Eberswalde, are the only springs which have any reputation. Climate and productions.] The climate is mild for the latitude; and has been improved by the draining of the large morasses. In summer the heat is often oppressive, the therm. occasionally reaching 88° and 90°. On the other hand a degree of cold 18° below zero has been observed. The average temp. at four different points in the prov., calculated from a series of observations, is as follows:- Brandenburg produces every species of corn; but very little wheat. The vegetables are good; and fruits of all kinds, and excellent grapes are raised, though not in sufficient quantity for making wine. Gardening is conducted with great skill in the neighbourhood of Berlin and Potsdam. Woad is an important article of culture. Estimating the entire surface at 15,800,000 morgens 6,700,000 m. were under cultivation in 1837; 3,500,000 m. were in forests; and 550,000 m. were unproductive land. The rearing of cattle is well-conducted; and the sheep have been greatly improved by crossing with merinoes. Government has likewise endeavoured to improve the breed of horses by the establishment of breeding-studs.—The manufactures of this province are considerable; but are confined to a few of the towns. The principal articles are wool, linen, cotton, silk, iron, tar, potash, fine cast iron-ware, glass, china, and earthenware.—There are few metals or minerals. Gypsum, alum, and coals are wrought. Population.] The pop. is a mixture of several races. The aborigines were Wendes, who, in process of time, blended with their conquerors the Saxons; but numerous colonists from different countries have also settled here. During the reign of Frederic II., 262 colonies were formed in the Mark, and about 12,000 foreign families settled here. The majority of the pop. is Protestant. Stein, estimating the total pop. at 1,336,000 in 1826, states that of these about 1,274,000 belonged to the Lutheran, the Reformed, or the Moravian church; 18,000 were Catholics; 307 Mennonites; and 8,500 Jews. In 1840, of a total pop. of 1,850,000, 25,000 were Catholics, and 13,747 Jews. The province of B. offers one of the principal points of German civilization, and the excellent educationary establishments here are admirably conducted. In 1837, of 338,344 children of an age to go to school in this prov. 283,760 were under instruction. Topography.] This prov. is now administratively divided into the regencies of Potsdam and Frankfort. The former comprises part of the Middle Mark, the Uckemark, the Priegnitz or Vormark, part of the lordships of Breskow and Storkow, the principality of Magdeburg, the lordship of Baruth, part of the Kur circle formerly comprised in Saxony, and the principality of Querfurt. Area 382.5 German or 8,122 English sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 1,100,000; in 1843, 1,135,335. — The regency of Frankfort comprises part of the Middle Mark, the New Mark, part of the lordships of Breskow and Storkow, the Suabian circle formerly belonging to the principality of Glogau, the Niederlausitz formerly belonging to Saxony, the bailiwicks of Seuftenberg and Finsterwalde, and part of the Kur circle. Area 348.5 German or 7,400 English sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 750,000; in 1843, 799,772.—The number of towns in the whole prov. is 138; of villages, 5,930. History.] This country -- from which the royal family of Prussia derive their origin—when first mentioned in history was occupied by the Suevi, or Suabes, the most numerous and most warlike of the German tribes known to Cæsar. Other German tribes—chiefly Longobards—mingled with them, or pushed them farther onwards, till all shared the fate of the other Northern German tribes in the great emigration which took place in the first centuries of the Christian era. The Wendes, or Slavonian tribes—among whom the Wilzes were the principal—built several towns, one of which was Brannibor, from which some derive the name Brandenburg. They fell under the power of Charlemagne in 789; but resumed their independence under his successors. The Saxon king, Henry, subdued them, and conquered Brannibor; after which, for the security of the Saxon frontier, grafen or counts were appointed in 921, who were the first margraves of Northern Saxony, or of the Wendish mark. During three centuries the Wendes maintained constant warfare with the German tribes bordering upon the Elbe, until the emperor Lothar gave the Northern mark to Albert the Bear, count of Ascania, who first assumed the title of margrave of Brandenburg. Albert conquered the rest of the marks, and either founded Berlin or at least raised it to the rank of a town. He was succeeded by his son Otto L whose descendants reigned till 1320, when the Ascanian line was extinguished in the person of the margrave Henry, and the emperor Louis of Bavaria gave Brandenburg to his son Louis. Since that period this country passed successively from one house to another, till the emperor Sigismund, in 1415, gave the mark of Brandenburg-to which the dignity of elector had been united—to Frederic of Hohenzollern, burgrave of Nürnberg, who had lent him 400,000 gold florins, and rendered him other important services. Frederic fixed his residence at Berlin, and was the ancestor of the present royal house of Prussia. The later history of this province is included in our historical notice of the kingdom of Prussia.

BRANDENBURG, an old town of the Prussian states, in the Middle Mark of B., the capital of the old electorate, and now of the circle of W Havelland, situated on the river Havel, which divides it into the old and new towns, 34 m. W by S of Berlin, and 38 m. NE of Magdeburg. The space between the two is a swampy district, covered with houses built on piles, from which it has received the name of Venice. Each town is surrounded by a wall. Both towns were united under one magistrate in 1716, but each retains its own possessions and revenues. The whole contains 1,400 houses, and 8 BRA cluding the garrison, 14,000 inhabitants. B. is the seat of a court-of-justice; and possesses a high school, several elementary schools, 5 hospitals, and a house-of-correction. Several branches of manufacture exist here in a flourishing condition. The number of individuals engaged in woollen cloth making is about 700. Its other manufactures are brandy, paper, beer, leather, and stockings. The only vestige of the ancient bishopric of B. is the chapter, which is composed of a provost, 6 capitulars, and 6 minor canons, who are all appointed by the king, and of noble families.
BRANDENBURG, a small town of Prussia, in the circle of Königsberg, on the Frischehaff, at the mouth of the Frischings. Pop. 1,000.

BRANDENBURG (New), a town in the grandduchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, 70 m. N of Berlin, and 17 m. N by E of New Strelitz, on the Tollensee. It is well-built, and has a castle and a large townhall. Pop. 6,000. The principal articles of manufacture are brandy, woollen-cloths, and printed cottons.
BRANDENBURG, a picturesque village, cap. of Mead co., in the state of Kentucky, U. S., on the S bank of the Ohio, 94 m. SW of Frankfort.
BRANDENFELS, a village of Kur-Hessen, in Lower Hessen, on the Werra, SE of Sontra.
BRANDESTON, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. SE of Reepham. Area 580 acres. Pop. 137.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 31 m. SW of Framlingham. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 555.
BRANDEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Damvillers. Pop. 1,000.
BRANDHOF, a village of Styria, in the gov. of Laybach, and circle of Bruck, on the Seeberg. In its vicinity is a fine castle.
BRANDHOLM, a town of Denmark, in the prov. of Jutland, in the amt and 23 m. NW of Weile.
BRANDIS, a village of Saxony, in the circle and 10 m. E of Leipzig, 4 m. NNW of Grimma. Pop. 1,180. It possesses manufactories of fine thread and linen. The environs afford excellent pasturage.

BRANDO, a canton, commune, and town of the island of Corsica, arrond. of Bastia. The cant. comprises 3 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 2,833. The town is on the NE coast, 5 m. N of Bastia. Pop. 1,189.
BRANDON WITH BYSHOTTLES, a township in the p. of Brancepeth, co.-palatine of Durham, 3 m. WSW of Durham. Area 3,460 acres. Pop. 467.
BRANDON, a township in the p. of Eglingham, Northumberland, 81 m. ESE of Wooler, on the N side of the Breamish. Pop. 147.

BRANDON, a parish and market-town in the co. of Suffolk, 78 m. NNE of London, and 42 m. NNE of Ipswich, on the Little Ouse or B. river. Area of p. 5,570 acres. Pop. 2,002.—The town of B. has a considerable trade in corn, malt, coal, bricks, iron, and timber. The dukes of Suffolk took their name from this place; it now gives the title of duke to the Hamilton family.—B. is the only place in England in which gun-flints are now made to any considerable extent. During the war, and before the invention of percussion-caps, when the demand for gun-flints was much greater than it is at present, some were made at Lewisham, Maidstone, Purfleet, Greenhithe, and Northfleet; but none are made there at present. The masses of flint from which the gun-flints are made at B. are obtained from a common about I m. SE of the town. The workers sink a shaft down about 6 ft., then proceed about 3 ft. horizontally, and sink another shaft lower down into the chalk about the same depth of 6 ft.; and so proceed sometimes to the depth of 30 ft. By making their shafts only about 6 ft. in depth, they are able to descend, and churches besides the cathedral church; and, in- | hand up the stone from one stage to another without the aid of machinery. In a descent of 30 ft., they generally find three floors of flint, and sometimes four. At every floor of flint a horizontal excavation is made for several yards. The flint is in large blocks, in form like the septaria stone.—Feltwell-fen, in this parish, comprising an area of 2,400 acres, is drained by a steam-engine of 20 horse-power.
BRANDON AND BRETSFORD, a hamlet in the p. of Wolston, co. of Warwick, 51 m. ESE of Coventry, on the N bank of the Avon, intersected by the London and Birmingham railway. Area 940 acres. Pop. 400.
BRANDON (LITTLE), a p. in Norfolk, 5 m. NNW of Wymondham. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 222.

BRANDON, a township of Rutland co., in the state of Vermont, U.S., 48 m. SW of Montpelier. It is watered by Otter creek and Mill river. The soil, excepting on the banks of the Otter, possesses little fertility. Bog iron ore and marble are found in the locality. Pop. in 1840, 2,194.—Also a village, cap. of Rankin co., in the state of Massachusetts, 13 m. E of Jackson, with which it is connected by a railroad.—Also a township of Oakland co., in the state of Michigan, drained by the head streams of the Flint river. Pop. 442. — Also a township of 1,322. Franklin co., in the state of New York, 10 m. SW of Albany. It is hilly, and to a great extent covered with wood. Several lakes occur in its southern dist., and it is intersected by the Saranac, St. Regis, and Racket rivers. Pop. 531.—Also a township in the co. of Berthier, in Lower Canada. Area 40,000 acres. Pop. 20.

BRANDON, a mountain, headland, bay, and village, on the N coast of co. Kerry. The mountain occupies a broad peninsula between Smerwick harbour on the W and Brandon bay on the E; it is situated 7 m. E by N of Cape Sybil, the NW extremity of Corkaguiney peninsula; and has an alt. above sea-level of 3,126 ft. The headland, or B. head, which is simply a seaward spur of the mountain, 4 m. NE of the mountain's summit, screens the W side of the entrance of B. bay. This bay is nearly 4 m. wide at the entrance, penetrates the land to the extent of about 3 m., describes nearly a semicircle in its outline, with an angular addition on the SW, and is separated from Tralee bay by only a narrow peninsula. B. village, a fishing and a coast-guard station, is situated on the W side of the bay.
BRANDON, a mountain of co. Kilkenny, 2 m. W. from the Barrow, a little above St. Mullin's Tideend, and 2½ m. NE of the Nore at Innistingue. Alt. above sea-level 1,693 ft.
BRANDON HOUSE, a station of British N America, on the r. bank of the Assiniboine, 110 m. WSW of the junction of that river with Red river.
BRANDSOE, or Brands, an island of Denmark, in the duchy of Sleswig, bailiwick of Hadersleben, in the Little Belt, about 1 m. from the coast, in N lat. 55° 23′, E long. 9° 44′.
BRANDSBURTON, or BRANDESBURTON, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NE of Beverley, comprising the townships of B. and Moortown. Area of p. 3,140 acres; of t. 2,680. Pop. of p. 718; of t. 684.

BRANDT, a township of Erie co., in the state of New York, U. S., on Lake Erie, 20 m. SSW of Buffalo. The surface is hilly, and the soil, consisting of moist clay and gravelly loam, is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,088.
BRANDY, a township of William's co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 351.
BRANDY POTS, three islets in the estuary of the St. Lawrence, to the NW of Hare island, in N lat. 47° 52′ 36″.

BRANDYWINE, a township of Hancock co., in the state of Iowa, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 693.—Also a BRA township of Chester co., in the state of Pennsylvania, watered by the Brandywine and Beaver creeks. Pop. 1,672.—Also a hundred of Newcastle co., in the state of Delaware. Pop. 3,387.

BRANDYWINE CREEK, a river of the United States, which takes its rise on the confines of Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, crosses Chester co., and uniting with Christiana creek, enters the state of Delaware, where it falls into the river of that name, forming at the junction the harbour of Wilmington. It affords excellent water-power, and is navigable at high tide to Brandywine village.
BRANESTI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Upper Wallachia, on the r. bank of the Jalomnitza, 10 m. NNW of Tergovist, and 55 m. NW of Bukarest.

BRANFORD, a township of New Haven co., in the state of Connecticut, U. S., on Long Island sound, 7 m. E of New Haven. It is drained by a river of the same name, which is to a small extent navigable for small vessels, and a fine pond, called Saltonstale's lake, occurs near its W confines. It is hilly, but is generally fertile. Thimble and Indian islands, in Long Island sound, belong to this township. Pop. in 1840,
BRANGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Louhans. Pop. 1,616.

BRANNA, or Bran, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Bidschow, 60 m. N of Neu-Bidschow, and 3 m. E of Starkenbach. Pop. 1,937. It contains a castle, and has manufactories of fine thread, linen, and cambric, which are reckoned the best in the kingdom.

BRANNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, arrond. of Libourne. The cant. comprises 19 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 10,238. The town is on the l. bank of the Dordogne, 8 m. SSE of Libourne. Pop. 560.

BRANNEBURG, a village of Bavaria, at the foot of a mountain of the same name, in the circle of Ober-Bavaria, district of Rosenheim, 4 m. from Fischbach. Pop. 166. It contains an ancient seignorial castle of the counts of Preising.
BRANSAT, a commune of France, in the prov. of Allier, cant. of Saint-Pourcain. Pop. 1,164.
BRANSBY, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. ENE of Easingwold. Area 2,700 acres. Pop. 304.
BRANSCOMBE, a parish in Devon, 43 m. E of Sidmouth. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 956.
BRANSFORD, a chapelry in the p. of Powick, in Worcestershire, 41 m. WSW of Worcester. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 277.
BRANSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Grodno, district of Bielsk, on the r. bank of the Nourtchik, 30 m. SW of Bialstok. Pop. 1,350.
BRANSTON, a parish in Lincolnshire, in the liberty and 4 m. SE by S of Lincoln. Pop. 1,152.— Also a p. in Leicestershire, 8 m. NE by N of Melton- Mowbray. Area 960 acres. Pop. 333.
BRANSZCZYK, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Plock, obwod and 27 m. from Pultusk. Pop. 390.
BRANTHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 72 SSW of Ipswich, on the N bank of the Stour. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 404.
BRANTHWAITE, a township in Dean p., in Cumberland, 6 m. SW of Cockermouth. Pop. 300.
BRANTINGHAM, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SE by E of South Cave. Area 3,040 acres. Pop. 635.

BRANTOME, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Périgueux. The cant. comprises 11 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 11,734. The town is on the Dronne, 15 m. N of Périgueux. Pop. 2,722.
BRANTON, a township in the p. of Eglingham, Northumberland, 9 m. SSE of Wooler. Pop. 119.
BRANZI, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Bergamo, district and 6 m. NNE of La Piazza, on the r. bank of the Brembo. Pcp. 300. It has an annual fair.
BRAONE, a town of Venetian Lorabardy, on an affluent of the Oglio, 3 m. NNE of Breno.-Also a town of Greece, in the prov. of Attica, 13 m. E by S of Athens, on the Erasinos.
BRAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Barjols. Pop. 1,477. The environs afford considerable quantities of wine.

BRAS (LE), a river of Lower Canada, which takes its rise in the parish of St. Gervais, crosses a portion of St. Michael seignory, and falls into the Riviere du Sud in the seignory of St. Vallier. It is altogether unnavigable.
BRAS DE L'EST, a river of Lower Canada, in the seignory of L'Islet.

BRAS D'OR, a spacious inlet which penetrates the SE corner of Cape Breton island for about 50 m., branching into numerous bays and creeks. It has two entrances, termed the Great and the Little Bras d'Or, and divided by Boulardrie island. The shores of the Little Bras d'Or are chiefly settled by emigrants from the Hebrides. That part of the Bras d'Or which forms the body of the inlet, exclusive of its arms, is about 20 m. in length, by 15 m. in breadth, and is in some places 60 fath. deep. It abounds with cod, which are caught here at all seasons.
BRAS DU OUEST, a river of Lower Canada, in the township of Tring.
BRASHER FALLS, a village of Brasher township, St. Lawrence co., in the state of New York, U. S., on the Deer river, which here affords extensive water-power. Pop. of t. in 1840, 2,118.
BRASIL. See BRAZIL.
BRASLAV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 90 m. NE of Vilna, district and 20 m. NE of Vidzy, on a lake.
BRASPART, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, cant. of Pleyben. Pop. 2,640.
BRASSA. See Bressay.

BRASSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Foix. Pop. 1,435.—Also a com. in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Montagrier. Pop. 1,910. The environs afford good red wine. — Also a com. in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Jumeaux, on the Allier, 10 m. SSE of Issoire. Coal abounds in the environs; and boat-building is extensively carried on for the transit of the produce of the coal-mines to Paris and Orleans. Curious crystallizations are found in the vicinity of the castle of Brassac.—Also a com. in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, cant. of Bourg-de-Visa. Pop. 1,194. BRASSAC, Brassac-de-Castelnau, or Bras-

SAC-DE-BELFOURTE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. of Castres.— The cant. comprises 6 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 10,698.—The com. and town is situated in a pleasant valley, on the Agout, 15 m. E of Castres. Pop. 1,875. It has considerable manufactories of cotton fabrics.
BRASSCHAET, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 6 m. NE of Antwerp. Pop. 2,222. It possesses a considerable commerce in madder.
BRASSEMPOUY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Amou. Pop. 1,082. The environs produce good wine.
BRASSE-PULO, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, near the NW extremity of the island of Sumatra, in N lat. 5° 50', E long. 95° 10'. BRA borne, Derbyshire, 4 m. NW of Wirksworth. Pop. 776.
BRASS (LITTLE), an island of the Virgin group, West Indies, near the N coast of the island of St. Thomas, in N lat. 18° 25', and W long. 65'.
BRASSOS. See Brazos.

BRASS TOWN, a town of W Africa, on a creek in the delta of the Niger, about 30 m. E of the mouth of the Nun. It is a few miles distant from the sea, and carries on a considerable trade in palm-oil on the Niger, as far up as Aboh, and also between the rivers Nun and Bonny. It consists, properly speaking, of two towns of nearly equal size, each containing about 1,000 inhabitants, and built opposite each other, and within the distance of 80 yards, on a kind of basin, formed by a number of rivulets entering it from the Niger. The soil in the vicinity is marshy, and covered with mangroves. The principal employment of the people consists in making salt, fishing, boiling oil, and trading to the Eboe country.—Lander.
BRASSU, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, near the r. bank of the Orbe, in the Val de Joux, 21 m. W of Lausanne.
BRASSY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Lormes. Pop. 1,663.
BRASTED, a parish in Kent, 4 m. NW of Seven Oaks. Area 4,630 acres. Pop. 1,130.
BRATHAY, a river of Westmoreland, flowing through Great Langdale, and uniting with the Rothay, 1 m. before the united streams fall into Windermere lake.

BRATISH, a lake of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Moldavia, dist. of Galatz, near the junction of the Pruth and Danube,—on the N and SE it is, connected with the former, and on the SW with the latter.
BRATSK, sometimes called SAINT PETER, a town of Russia in Asia, in the gov. and 250 m. NNW of Irkutsk, dist. of Nij Udinsk, at the confluence of the Aka with the Angara. It is inhabited by Tunguses.

BRATSLAV, a town of Russia in Europe, the cap. of a dist. of the same name, in the gov. of Podolia, on the r. bank of the Boug, 18 m. W of Gaisin, and 65 m. ENE of Kamienetz. Pop. 2,600. It is defended by old walls, and gives its name to the archiepiscopal diocese of Podolia and Bratslav. This town was founded in 1331 by the dukes of Koryatowicz, and it was formerly cap. of the Polish palatinate of the same name.—The dist. of B. is watered by the Boug, and is intersected by several ranges of hills. It is very fertile, and is, to a considerable extent, covered with forests. It possesses some manufactures.
BRATTELEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. ESE of Basle.
BRATTEN, a village of Denmark, in the prov. of N Jutland, amt of Hioring, on the shore of the Kattegat, 6 m. NNW of Fladstrand.
BRATTLE, an island of the S Pacific, in the Galapagos group, in S lat. 1° 5', and W long. 90° 55'.
BRATTLEBOROUGH, formerly called FORT

DUMMER, a township of Windham co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., on the W bank of the Connecticut, at the junction of the Whetstone, 95 m. WNW of Boston. It is the oldest and one of the most flourishing settlements in the state. Pop. in 1840, 2,624.
BRATTLEBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 7 m. NNE of Lincoln. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 168.
BRATTON, a chapelry in the p. and 31 m. ENE of Westbury, Wilts. Pop. 729.
BRATTON-CLOVELLY, a parish of Devon, 8 m. WSW of Oakhampton. Area 8,200 acres. Pop 870.
BRATTON-FLEMING, a parish of Devon, 6 m.
BRASSINGTON, a chapelry in the p. of Brad- | ENE of Barnstaple. Area 4,370 acres. Pop. 708.
BRATTON-SEYMOUR, or St. Maur, a parish of Somerset, 21 m. NW of Wincanton. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 103.
BRATZ, or BROYCE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Posen, circle and 10 m. SSE of Meseritz. Pop. in 1837, 1,475. It contains two parish churches; and has manufactories of cloth and breweries.
BRATZA, or PRATZA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Bosnia, sanjak of Trawnik, in a valley, on an affluent of the Drin, 20 m. SE of Bosna-Serai.

BRAUBACH, a town of Nassau, cap. of a bailiwick of the same name, on the r. bank of the Rhine, 7 m. W of Nassau, and 4 m. SSE of Lahnstein. Pop. 1,400. It has copper and silver founderies; and in the environs are the mineral springs and baths of Dinkhold. In the vicinity are the castles of Phillipsburg and Marsburg; the latter stands on a mountain which overlooks the town, and is of great strength. The bailiwick comprises 18 com., and contains a pop. of 8,585.
BRAUCHITSDORF, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, and 10 m. N of Liegnitz.
BRAUD, a village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of St. Ciers-la-Lande, 8 m. NNE of Blaye. Pop. 1,200.
BRAUGHIN, a parish of Hertfordshire, 7 m. NNE of Ware. Area 4,300 acres. Pop. 1,385.

BRAUGLIO (MONTE), a mountain on the SE confines of the cant. of Grisons, 8 m. N of Bormio, alt. 9,777 ft., forming one of the loftiest summits of the Rhætian Alps. At its SE extremity is the source of the Adda, and the pass of Monte Stelvio. Alt. 9,272 ft. This mountain was the Juga Rhætica of Tacitus. There is a passage over it—little used now -from the Valteline to the valley of the Adige; and a road, formed in a series of short traverses, leads down from it to the Wurmserloch.

BRAUNAU, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Upper Austria, circle of Ried, 30 m. SW of Passau, on the r. bank of the Inn, which is here crossed by a bridge. Pop. 2,000. It possesses manufactories of common cloth, linen, and paper, and has celebrated breweries. It was ceded to Austria by Bavaria in 1779.—Also a town of Bohemia, cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 35 m. NE of Könniggrätz. Pop. 3,019. It contains a rich Benedictine abbey, founded in 1331, and rebuilt early in the 18th century, and, in connection with it, a royal gymnasium; and possesses extensive manufactories of scarlet and fine cloth—of which, in 1835, about 5,000 pieces were furnished for the Turkey market— and linen. It has also spinning-mills, soap and dye works, tanneries, and fullers-mills.
BRAUNCEWELL AND DUNSBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 41 m. NNW of Sleaford. Area 3,470 acres. Pop. 125.

BRAUNFELS, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, regency and 35 m. ENE of Coblentz and circle of Wetzlar, near the l. bank of the Lahn. Pop. in 1837, 1,464. It contains an ancient fortified castle, the residence of the princes of Solms- Braunfels.
BRAUNHIRSCHEN, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, in the district and near the city of Vienna. Pop. 3,700.
BRAUNISTA. See BROUNISTA.

BRAUNLAGE, a town of Brunswick, in the dist. and 16 m. WSW of Blankenburg, and 50 m. S of Brunswick, on the r. bank of the Bode, near its source. It has some iron forges, and manufactories of Prussian blue, and wooden-ware.
BRAUNLINGEN, a town of Baden, cap. of the bailiwick of the same name, in the circle of the See, near the l. bank of the Brege, 7 m. S of Villingen, and 10 m. ENE of Neustadt. Pop. 1,447. ~ BRA

BRAUNSBACH, or Braunspach, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Jaxt, about 6 m. SE of Künzelsau, on the r. bank of the Kocher. Pop. 665. It has a castle; and forms part of the barony of the prince of Hohenlohe-Waldenbourg- Jaxtberg.

BRAUNSBERG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of E Prussia, cap. of a circle of the same name, in the regency and 35 m. SW of Königsberg, on the Passarge, which divides the old from the newer dist. of the town. Pop. in 1817, 5,786; in 1837, 7,746. It contains 6 churches, a lyceum, a university, a diocesan seminary, a normal school, and a monastery; and has manufactories of cloth, linen and ribbon, tanneries, and yarn spinning-mills. Corn, linen, thread, and timber, form the chief articles of its commerce.— B. was founded in 1225, and was formerly the cap. of the bishopric of Ermeland.—The circle of B. comprises an area of 150 sq. m.; and contains a pop. of 26,613. BRAUNSBERG, BRAUNSPERG, or BRUSSBERG, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 40 m. NE of Prerau, and 25 m. NE of Weisskirchen. Pop. 2,781. It has cloth manufactories, which were formerly very extensive.

BRAUNSDORF, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Dresden, bailiwick and 6 m. W of Freiburg, on the Striegiss. Pop. in 1834, 1,673. It contains an orphan asylum, and juvenile house of correction. Silver and garnet are found in the environs.

BRAUNSEIFEN, or BRUNZAISA, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 21 m. NNE of Olmutz, seignory and 8 m. NE of Eulenberg. Pop. 2,062. It contains an hospital, and has extensive manufactories of linen and ironmongery, and a brewery.
BRAUNSTON, a parish and township of Rutland, 2½ m. WSW of Odiham. Area 3,250 acres. Pop. 443.
BRAUNSTON, or Brandestone, a parish of Northamptonshire, 3 m. NW of Daventry. Area 3,930 acres. Pop. 1,469.
BRAUNSTONE, a chapelry in the p. of Glenfield. Leicestershire, 2½ m. WSW of Leicester. Area 1,610 acres. Pop. 195.
BRAUNTON, a parish of Devon, 5 m. WNW of Barnstaple. Area 7,010 acres. Pop. 2,274.
BRAUS (Col-DE), a pass over a wild and rugged acclivity in the Sardinian Alps, between the valley of the Roya and the Paglione in Nice. Alt 3,845 ft. above sea-level.
BRAUWEILER, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, circle and 7 m. W of Cologne. Pop. 400. It contains an ancient Benedictine monastery, now used as a house-of-correction.
BRAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Monthermie. Pop. 1,299.
BRAUX-SAINT-LOUIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Saint-Ciers-la-Lande. Pop. 1,475.

BRAVA, a town of E Africa, in the Mukdeesha district, coast of Zanguebar, 110 m. SW of Magadoxo, in N lat. 1° 12'. Pop. 2,000. It is situated on a bay, at the mouth of a river. It has an active trade. The chief articles of export are gold and silver. In the 15th cent. B. was the cap. of a republic, consisting of Arabian refugees, but subsequently became a Portuguese settlement. It has since recovered its independence, and has placed itself under British protection.

BRAVA, or St. JEAN, an island of the N Atlantic, in the Cape de Verde group, 12 m. WSW of Fogo island, in N lat. 14° 50', and 24° 47' E long. Pop. 500, all Blacks. It rises to a considerable height, but is almost constantly covered by a dense atmosphere. On the W side it affords anchorage for small vessels. Its chief productions are Indian corn, beans, orchilla, wine, and saltpetre.—Above 5 m. from its NE extremity lie the Rombo or Romes islands.
BRAVA (ISLA), the southernmost and largest of a series of low swampy kays which form the NW coast of Golfo Triste, on the coast of Venezuela, in N lat. 11° 47'.

BRAVES, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the Amazon, above the mouth of the Tocantins. It is, says Edwards, "one of those little towns that have grown up since the active demand for rubber, of which the surrounding district yields vast quantities. It is a small collection of houses partly thatched and partly of mud, stationed anywhere, regardless of streets or right lines. We were struck at B.," Edwards continues, "by the appearance of some Portuguese boys, whose teeth had been sharpened in the Indian manner. The custom is quite fashionable among that class who come over seeking their fortunes, they evidently considering it as a sort of naturalisation. The blade of a knife or razor is laid across the edge of the tooth, and by a slight blow and dexterous turn a piece is chipped off on either side. All the front teeth, above and below, are thus served; and they give a person a very odd, and to a stranger, a very disagreeable appearance. For some days after the operation is performed the patient is unable to eat or drink without severe pain; but the teeth soon lose their sensitiveness, and then seem to decay no faster than the others."
BRAVO (Rio). See Grande del Norte.
BRAWBY, a township of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NNW of Malton. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 218.
BRAWDY, a parish of Pembroke, S Wales, 6 m. E of St. David's. Pop. 767.
BRAXTED (GREAT), a parish in Essex, 2 m. E by N of Witham. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 410.
